text: This is a video presentation of a laparoscopic distal pancreatectomy and splenectomy. This was a seventy-year-old patient with a background history of laparotomy for bowel cancer who was found to have a twenty millimetre cyst in the body of the pancreas on a CT scan.

The surgeon begins the procedure by dividing adhesions and mobilizing the splenic flexure. The gastroesplenic ligament and short gastric vessels are divided, allowing entry into the lesser sac. Dissection proceeds along the greater curvature of the stomach, preserving the gastroepiploic arcade, to expose the body of the pancreas.

The surgeon continues lateral dissection to expose the tail of the pancreas. The peritoneum is incised along the inferior border of the pancreas, revealing the confluence between the inferior mesenteric and splenic veins.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic ultrasound assessment. The splenic artery is gently dissected and looped with a red sling. The body of the pancreas is lifted, creating a tunnel, and controlled with a white sling. The splenic vein is isolated and looped with a blue sling. The splenic artery is divided between haemlocks, followed by the division of the splenic vein in a similar manner.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic stapler to transect the body of the pancreas, ensuring clear margins. The pancreatic attachments to the retroperitoneum are divided while maintaining traction on the pancreas. The remaining splenic attachments are divided from medial to lateral to disconnect the specimen. The specimen is placed in a large endo-catch, and a small five-centimeter incision is made over the previous laparotomy scar for specimen extraction.
